subject: English Language
subtopic: Economic recovery and government fiscal policy post-pandemic
question:
The last few months have been great for the economy. New cases have fallen, and economic activity is racing back
to pre-pandemic levels. After a c.24% contraction in the quarter ending June 2020, we expect GDP to grow by a
positive 1.8% in the quarter ending December 2020.

This is quite a sharp turnaround in a short period. A careful look suggests that a key driver of the rebound has
been pent-up goods demand. As the lockdown ended, the production of consumer non-durables shot up,
followed______________(I)___________. A large mountain of household financial savings funded this rebound.

Alas, we also find that goods demand is back at pre-pandemic levels and may not be the key driver of a continued
rebound. Thankfully, pent-up services demand can play that role. Still 25% below normal, services can get a shot
in the arm as herd immunity rises, in part led by vaccine roll-out. GDP growth is likely to be strong for the next
few quarters, rising from -6.3% y-o-y in FY21 to 11.2% y-o-y in FY22.

But then, what next? By definition, pent-up demand is a one-time driver of growth. Once services demand is back
at pre-pandemic levels, say by end 2021, what will drive growth? It is possible that the scars the pandemic leaves
behind will begin to show up around that same time, presenting a double whammy for growth. And this is where
the centre stepped in with the budget. It tried to introduce a new narrative for medium-term growth, namely
capital expenditure. In particular, it introduced the following:

The capex budget was raised by 0.8% of GDP over two years (FY21 and FY22). In fact, only after adjusting for the
higher capex multipliers is the FY22 fiscal impulse positive.

The government did not impose any new taxes/cesses, nor did it make changes in capital gains tax. Our previous
work has shown that policy stability tends to crowd-in private sector capex.

The government outline plans to create two new institutions, a bad bank and a DFI, although much will depend
on the design and implementation over time.

On Feb 5, RBI outlined its role in this new narrative–not being the main driver of growth as it was in 2020, but
playing a supportive role and helping it through its larger-than-expected market borrowing.

RBI will have to tread the fine line between normalising liquidity (especially with inflation likely to be north of
the 4% target over the next year) and maintaining orderly conditions in the bond and FX markets. Liquidity
switching could help. For instance, it could use the space freed up by the reversal in CRR cut for bond purchases.
Or, in the face of a rising trade deficit and falling BoP surplus, it could focus more on bond purchases than dollar
purchases.RBI is expected to start raising the reverse repo rate in 2H2021, the repo rate may remain unchanged
at 4% over the foreseeable future, doing its bit for keeping interest rates as low as possible.

Q1. According to the passage, what can be said about the economy after the pandemic? (2 Marks)

option.A: New cases have risen, and economic activity is highly influenced by it.
option.B: Still 85% below normal, services can get a shot in the arm as herd immunity rises, in part led by vaccine roll-out.
option.C: Economic activity is racing back to pre-pandemic levels.

explanation: The first paragraph of the passage explicitly states: 'New cases have fallen, and economic activity is racing back to pre-pandemic levels.' Option C matches this statement. Option B mentions 85% instead of the 25% stated in the text.

Q2. The line emboldened in the given passage may or may not have an error. If there is an error, find the part that
contains the error or choose ‘no error’ as your answer.

The government outline plans (A) / to create two new institutions, a bad bank and a DFI (B) / although much will
depend on the design (C) /and implementation over time. (D) (2 Marks)

option.B: to create two new institutions, a bad bank and a DFI
option.C: although much will depend on the design

explanation: The phrase 'The government outline plans' is grammatically incorrect. It should be 'The government outlined plans' to match the past tense context of the reporting.

Or, in the face of a rising trade deficit and falling BoP surplus, it could focus more on bond purchases than dollar purchases.RBI is expected to start raising the reverse repo rate in 2H2021, the repo rate may remain unchanged at 4% over the foreseeable future, doing its bit for keeping interest rates as low as possible.
\nQ4. Which of the following sentences is incorrect according to the passage? (2 Marks)

option.A: The capex budget was raised by 0.8% of GDP over two years.

subject: English Language
question:
Maharashtra is facing a water crisis of unprecedented proportions. After years of drought, the river currents have 
eebed, water in dams and reservoirs have depleted and over-exploitation of groundwater has raised concerns 
regarding the long-term availability of water. 

Meanwhile, media reports claim IT firms in Chennai are asking employees to work from home. The reason is that 
they don’t have enough water to sustain their operations. It hasn’t rained for almost 200 days in the city and it 
may not get adequate rain to get over the water crisis for the next 3 months. 

In North India, the people of arid Thar Desert of Rajasthan are spending Rs. 2500 for getting 2500 litres of water 
which they share with their cattle. With Punjab facing the threat of desertification and the state struggling to 
break away from the wheat-paddy cycle, farmers in the state have been adopting a decade-old scheme to utilise 
underground pipeline system for irrigation. 

In light of this crisis, Central government on its part has created a Jal Shakti Ministry under a full-fledged cabinet 
minister to resolve the water crisis but a lot more needs to be done. There is a huge dependence on monsoon rains 
to replenish most of India’s important water sources such as underground aquifers, lakes, rivers, and reservoirs. 
But monsoon is vulnerable to factors such as climate change, El-Nino, etc. 

Certain regions have surplus amounts of water for their need while others face perennial droughts for most of 
the year. For instance, Drought is a recurrent phenomenon in Andhra Pradesh where no district is entirely free of 
droughts. Rajasthan is one of the most drought-prone areas of India. 

Population growth, industrialization , rapid urbanisation, rising needs of irrigation and increase in domestic 
water usage have accelerated the demand for water. Since urbanization increases in India at a rapid pace = water 
demand will increase rapidly as city dwellers consume more water than rural people. 

Currently, about 285 million or 33% of India’s total population resides in urban areas. By 2050 this figure will 
reach 50%. Rapid urbanisation is adding to the water scarcity issue in the country. 

Presence of buildings, tar, and cement roads = even if a city like Mumbai gets good rains, the rainwater is not 
retained in the area as the water is not allowed to percolate underground. 

Therefore, water required for cities is largely drawn from neighbouring villages and far-off rivers and lakes = 
threatening the availability in those areas. 

Large cities also generate large quantities of urban sewage which pollutes the freshwater sources and ocean 
waters. However, only about 20% of urban wastewater is currently treated globally. In India, the figure is even 
lower. 

A Niti Aayog report predicted that water demand will be twice the present supply by 2030 and India could lose 
up to 6% of its GDP during that time. Water shortages are hurting India’s capacity to generate electricity because 
40% of thermal power plants are located in areas where water scarcity is high.Indian agriculture is heavily 
dependent on monsoon (not dependable) + Ineffective agricultural practices in irrigated areas = Water stress in 
agriculture = Poor Cultivation = Farmer suicides. Not only farmers are affected by the water crisis, urban dwellers 
in cities and towns across India are also facing a never seen before drinking water scarcity. In India, there are 
conflicts between Karnataka and Tamil Nadu over sharing of Cauvery waters, between Gujarat and Madhya 
Pradesh over sharing of Narmada waters, between Andhra Pradesh and Telangana over sharing of Krishna 
waters, etc. 

Q5. According to the passage, which of the following sentences indicate the impact of water crisis? (2 Marks) 

(a) It is affecting the electricity generation capacity of India as the thermal power plants need water to generate electricity. 

(b) About 20% of urban wastewater is currently treated globally which shows a lower figure, 

(c) Rising needs of irrigation and increase in domestic water usage have accelerated water crisis in India. 

(d) It has affected the coal mines in India and production of coal has reduced drastically. 

(e) None of the above
explanation: According to the passage, water shortages are hurting India’s capacity to generate electricity because 40% of thermal power plants are located in areas where water scarcity is high. Option (a) accurately reflects this impact mentioned in the text.

Q6. Choose the word which is most opposite to the term ‘vulnerable’ highlighted in the passage? (1 Mark)
explanation: The word 'vulnerable' means exposed to the possibility of being attacked or harmed. 'Resilient' means able to withstand or recover quickly from difficult conditions, making it the most appropriate opposite in this context.

question:

\nIn North India, the people of arid Thar Desert of Rajasthan are spending Rs. 2500 for getting 2500 litres of water which they share with their cattle. With Punjab facing the threat of desertification and the state struggling to break away from the wheat-paddy cycle, farmers in the state have been adopting a decade-old scheme to utilise underground pipeline system for irrigation.
\nIn light of this crisis, Central government on its part has created a Jal Shakti Ministry under a full-fledged cabinet minister to resolve the water crisis but a lot more needs to be done. There is a huge dependence on monsoon rains to replenish most of India’s important water sources such as underground aquifers, lakes, rivers, and reservoirs. But monsoon is vulnerable to factors such as climate change, El-Nino, etc.
\nPopulation growth, industrialization, rapid urbanisation, rising needs of irrigation and increase in domestic water usage have accelerated the demand for water. Since urbanization increases in India at a rapid pace = water demand will increase rapidly as city dwellers consume more water than rural people.
\nCurrently, about 285 million or 33% of India’s total population resides in urban areas. By 2050 this figure will reach 50%. Rapid urbanisation is adding to the water scarcity issue in the country.
\nPresence of buildings, tar, and cement roads = even if a city like Mumbai gets good rains, the rainwater is not retained in the area as the water is not allowed to percolate underground.
\nTherefore, water required for cities is largely drawn from neighbouring villages and far-off rivers and lakes = threatening the availability in those areas.
\nLarge cities also generate large quantities of urban sewage which pollutes the freshwater sources and ocean waters. However, only about 20% of urban wastewater is currently treated globally. In India, the figure is even lower.
\nA Niti Aayog report predicted that water demand will be twice the present supply by 2030 and India could lose up to 6% of its GDP during that time. Water shortages are hurting India’s capacity to generate electricity because 40% of thermal power plants are located in areas where water scarcity is high. Indian agriculture is heavily dependent on monsoon (not dependable) + Ineffective agricultural practices in irrigated areas = Water stress in agriculture = Poor Cultivation = Farmer suicides. Not only farmers are affected by the water crisis, urban dwellers in cities and towns across India are also facing a never seen before drinking water scarcity. In India, there are conflicts between Karnataka and Tamil Nadu over sharing of Cauvery waters, between Gujarat and Madhya Pradesh over sharing of Narmada waters, between Andhra Pradesh and Telangana over sharing of Krishna waters, etc.
\nQ7. Choose the word which is most similar to the term ‘dwellers’ highlighted in the passage?
explanation: The term 'dwellers' refers to people who live in a particular place. 'Inhabitants' is the most appropriate synonym for dwellers, as it refers to people or animals that live in or occupy a place.

subject: English Language
question:
Read the passage carefully and answer the questions given below it.
\nChina’s rising power is based on its remarkable economic success. Shanghai’s overall economy is currently growing at around 13% per year, thus doubling in size every five or six years. Everywhere there are start-ups, innovations, and young entrepreneurs hungry for profits. In a series of high-level meetings between Chinese and African officials, the advice that the African leaders received from the Chinese was sound, and more practical than they typically get from the World Bank. Chinese officials stress the crucial role of public investments, especially in agriculture and infrastructure, to lay the basis for private sector-led growth. In a hungry and poor rural economy, as China was in the 1970s and as most of Africa is today, a key starting point is to raise farm productivity. 
\nFarmers need the benefits of fertilizer, irrigation and high-yield seeds, all of which were a core part of China’s economical take off. Two other equally critical investments are also needed: roads and electricity, without which there cannot be a modern economy. Farmers might be able to increase their output, but it won’t be able to reach the cities, and the cities won’t be able to provide the countryside with inputs. 
\nThe government has taken pains to ensure that the electricity grids and transportation networks reach every village in China. 
\nChina is prepared to help Africa in substantial ways in agriculture, roads, power, health and education. And that is not an empty boast. Chinese leaders are prepared to share new high yield rice varieties, with their African counterparts and, all over Africa, China is financing and constructing basic infrastructure. 
\nThis illustrates what is wrong with the World Bank. The World Bank has often forgotten the most basic lessons of development, preferring to lecture the poor and force them to privatise basic infrastructure, which is untenable, rather than to help the poor to invest in infrastructure and other crucial sectors. The Banks’s failure began in the early 1980s when under the ideological sway of them American President and British Prime Minister it tried to get Africa and other poor regions to cut back or close down government investments and services.
explanation: This entry contains the passage provided for reading comprehension. No specific questions were extracted below this passage in the source text.

subject: English Language
subtopic: Developmental Economics and World Bank Policy
question:
For 25 years, the bank tries to get governments out of agriculture, leaving impoverished peasants to fend for themselves. The result has been a disaster in Africa, with farm productivity stagnant for decades. The bank also pushed for privatization of national health systems, water utilities, and road and power networks, and has grossly underfinanced these critical sectors.
\nThis extreme free-market ideology, also called “structural adjustment”, went against the practical lessons of development successes in China and the rest or Asia. Practical development strategy recognizes that public investments - in agriculture, health, education, and infrastructure- are necessary complements to private investments. The World Bank has instead wrongly seen such vital public investments as an enemy of private sector development. Whenever the banks’ ideology failed, it has blamed the poor for corruption, mismanagement, or lack of initiative.
\nInstead of focusing its attention on helping the poorest countries to improve their infrastructure, there has been a crusade against corruption. The good news is that African governments are getting the message on how to spur economic growth and are getting crucial help from China and other partners that are less wedded to extreme free-market ideology than the world Bank.
\nThey have declared their intention to invest in infrastructure, agriculture modernization, public health, and education. It is clear the Bank can regain its relevance only if it becomes practical once again, by returning its focus to financing public investments in priority sectors. If that happens, the Bank can still do justice to the bold vision of a world of shared prosperity that prompted its creation after World War II.

Farmers might be able to increase their output, but it won’t be able to reach the cities, and the cities won’t be able to provide the countryside with inputs.
\nThe government has taken pains to ensure that the electricity grids and transportation networks reach every village in China.
\nChina is prepared to help Africa in substantial ways in agriculture, roads, power, health and education. And that is not an empty boast. Chinese leaders are prepared to share new high yield rice varieties, with their African counterparts and, all over Africa, China is financing and constructing basic infrastructure.
\nThis illustrates what is wrong with the World Bank. The World Bank has often forgotten the most basic lessons of development, preferring to lecture the poor and force them to privatise basic infrastructure, which is untenable, rather than to help the poor to invest in infrastructure and other crucial sectors. The Banks’s failure began in the early 1980s when under the ideological sway of them American President and British Prime Minister it tried to get Africa and other poor regions to cut back or close down government investments and services.
\nQ9. What is the advice given by the author to the World Bank?

option.A: Criticize the World Bank for playing a crucial role in China’s development but neglecting Australia.
option.B: Make a case for the closure of the World Bank since it promotes US interests over those of other countries.
option.C: Illustrate how China can play a more concrete role in Africa.
option.D: Use China’s success as an example of the changes required in World ideology.

explanation: The passage argues that the World Bank should move away from its extreme free-market ideology and instead adopt more practical development strategies—specifically by focusing on public investments in agriculture, infrastructure, health, and education—similar to the successful developmental approach taken by China.

subject: English Language
subtopic: World Bank vs. Chinese Economic Model in Africa
question:
This extreme free-market ideology, also called “structural adjustment”, went against the practical lessons of development successes in China and the rest or Asia. Practical development strategy recognizes that public investments - in agriculture, health, education, and infrastructure- are necessary complements to private investments. The World Bank has instead wrongly seen such vital public investments as an enemy of private sector development. Whenever the banks’ ideology failed, it has blamed the poor for corruption, mismanagement, or lack of initiative.
\nInstead of focusing its attention on helping the poorest countries to improve their infrastructure, there has been a crusade against corruption. The good news is that African governments are getting the message on how to spur economic growth and are getting crucial help from China and other partners that are less wedded to extreme free-market ideology than the world Bank.
\nThey have declared their intention to invest in infrastructure, agriculture modernization, public health, and education. It is clear the Bank can regain its relevance only if it becomes practical once again, by returning its focus to financing public investments in priority sectors. If that happens, the Bank can still do justice to the bold vision of a world of shared prosperity that prompted its creation after World War II.
\nQ. According to the passage, what should the World Bank do to regain its relevance?

(a) Reduce the influence of the Africa and Britain in its functioning.
(b) Adopt a more practical ideology of structural adjustment.
(c) Change its ideology to one encouraging both public and private sector investment in basic infrastructure.
(d) Support China’s involvement in developing Africa.
(e) None of the above

option.A: Reduce the influence of the Africa and Britain in its functioning.
option.B: Adopt a more practical ideology of structural adjustment.
option.C: Change its ideology to one encouraging both public and private sector investment in basic infrastructure.
option.D: Support China’s involvement in developing Africa.

explanation: The passage explicitly states that the Bank can regain its relevance by becoming practical again and returning its focus to financing public investments in priority sectors, which are necessary complements to private investments.

subject: English Language
question:
China’s rising power is based on its remarkable economic success. Shanghai’s overall economy is currently growing at around 13% per year, thus doubling in size every five or six years. Everywhere there are start-ups, innovations, and young entrepreneurs hungry for profits. In a series of high-level meetings between Chinese and African officials, the advice that the African leaders received from the Chinese was sound, and more practical than they typically get from the World Bank. Chinese officials stress the crucial role of public investments, especially in agriculture and infrastructure, to lay the basis for private sector-led growth. In a hungry and poor rural economy, as China was in the 1970s and as most of Africa is today, a key starting point is to raise farm productivity.
\nFarmers need the benefits of fertilizer, irrigation and high-yield seeds, all of which were a core part of China’s economical take off. Two other equally critical investments are also needed: roads and electricity, without which there cannot be a modern economy. Farmers might be able to increase their output, but it won’t be able to reach the cities, and the cities won’t be able to provide the countryside with inputs.
\nThe government has taken pains to ensure that the electricity grids and transportation networks reach every village in China.
\nChina is prepared to help Africa in substantial ways in agriculture, roads, power, health and education. And that is not an empty boast. Chinese leaders are prepared to share new high yield rice varieties, with their African counterparts and, all over Africa, China is financing and constructing basic infrastructure.
\nThis illustrates what is wrong with the World Bank. The World Bank has often forgotten the most basic lessons of development, preferring to lecture the poor and force them to privatise basic infrastructure, which is untenable, rather than to help the poor to invest in infrastructure and other crucial sectors. The Banks’s failure began in the early 1980s when under the ideological sway of them American President and British Prime Minister it tried to get Africa and other poor regions to cut back or close down government investments and services.
\nFor 25 years, the bank tries to get governments out of agriculture, leaving impoverished peasants to fend for themselves. The result has been a disaster in Africa, with farm productivity stagnant for decades. The bank also pushed for privatization of national health systems, water utilities, and road and power networks, and has grossly underfinanced these critical sectors.
\nThis extreme free-market ideology, also called “structural adjustment”, went against the practical lessons of development successes in China and the rest or Asia. Practical development strategy recognizes that public investments - in agriculture, health, education, and infrastructure- are necessary complements to private investments. The World Bank has instead wrongly seen such vital public investments as an enemy of private sector development. Whenever the banks’ ideology failed, it has blamed the poor for corruption, mismanagement, or lack of initiative.
\nQ10. Choose the word which is most similar in meaning to ‘ untenable’. (1 Mark)
explanation: In the context of the passage, 'untenable' refers to the policy of privatizing basic infrastructure which the author considers to be flawed, unsustainable, or impossible to support/maintain. Therefore, synonyms like 'indefensible' or 'unjustifiable' are most similar in meaning.

subject: English Language
question:
China’s rising power is based on its remarkable economic success. Shanghai’s overall economy is currently 
growing at around 13% per year, thus doubling in size every five or six years. Everywhere there are start-ups, 
innovations, and young entrepreneurs hungry for profits. In a series of high-level meetings between Chinese and 
African officials, the advice that the African leaders received from the Chinese was sound, and more practical than 
they typically get from the World Bank. Chinese officials stress the crucial role of public investments, especially 
in agriculture and infrastructure, to lay the basis for private sector-led growth. In a hungry and poor rural 
economy, as China was in the 1970s and as most of Africa is today, a key starting point is to raise farm productivity. 
 
Farmers need the benefits of fertilizer, irrigation and high-yield seeds, all of which were a core part of China’s 
economical take off. Two other equally critical investments are also needed: roads and electricity, without which 
there cannot be a modern economy. Farmers might be able to increase their output, but it won’t be able to reach 
the cities, and the cities won’t be able to provide the countryside with inputs. 
 
The government has taken pains to ensure that the electricity grids and transportation networks reach every 
village in China. 
 
China is prepared to help Africa in substantial ways in agriculture, roads, power, health and education. And that 
is not an empty boast. Chinese leaders are prepared to share new high yield rice varieties, with their African 
counterparts and, all over Africa, China is financing and constructing basic infrastructure. 
 
This illustrates what is wrong with the World Bank. The World Bank has often forgotten the most basic lessons of 
development, preferring to lecture the poor and force them to privatise basic infrastructure, which is untenable, 
rather than to help the poor to invest in infrastructure and other crucial sectors. The Banks’s failure began in the 
early 1980s when under the ideological sway of them American President and British Prime Minister it tried to 
get Africa and other poor regions to cut back or close down government investments and services. 
 
For 25 years, the bank tries to get governments out of agriculture, leaving impoverished peasants to fend for 
themselves. The result has been a disaster in Africa, with farm productivity stagnant for decades. The bank also 
pushed for privatization of national health systems, water utilities, and road and power networks, and has grossly 
underfinanced these critical sectors. 
 
This extreme free-market ideology, also called “structural adjustment”, went against the practical lessons of 
development successes in China and the rest or Asia. Practical development strategy recognizes that public 
investments - in agriculture, health, education, and infrastructure- are necessary complements to private 
investments. The World Bank has instead wrongly seen such vital public investments as an enemy of private 
sector development. Whenever the banks’ ideology failed, it has blamed the poor for corruption, 
mismanagement, or lack of initiative.

Q11. Choose the word which is most opposite in meaning to ‘ crusade’.
explanation: A 'crusade' refers to a vigorous campaign for political, social, or religious change. In the context of the passage, it implies an aggressive, often single-minded pursuit or movement. 'Indefensible' acts as a logical opposite in a rhetorical sense within the context of examining policy failures, though 'crusade' usually implies a moral campaign, its opposite in this context reflects the lack of justifiable basis for such a rigid approach.

subject: English Language
question:
Hope and fear are marching in lockstep. The arrival of the vaccines represents the ______ (12) _______ ( gloom, murk, 
dawn) of hope. Yet, fears about mutations of the virus and how politics, which defines public policy, will mutate 
loom large across India’s political economy. Political rhetoric propels public expectations and the declaration by 
Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman recently of “a budget which is a budget like never before” will be tested 
against a billion aspirations. The challenge of ______ (13) _______ ( revival, amelioration, droop) and recovery is 
located in a parade of riveting contrasts. India, it is useful to remember, migrated from slowdown to lockdown. 
The contrast between the message beamed by stock indices and indices of the real economy are striking to say 
the least. In the period post the high of 2016-17, growth has _______ (14) ________ (limped , slid, stumped) 
consistently and stock valuations have risen just as consistently. GDP growth slid from 8-plus per cent to hover at 
around 4 per cent to a contraction in the wake of the pandemic, while the benchmark Sensex has shot up from 
around 29,000 points to over 47,000 points. How has the fall in growth impacted government _______ (15) _______ ( 
expenditure, asset, revenues)? In theory, drop in output would result in drop in revenues. That though is not 
necessarily true in the Indian context. This week, the government informed India that the Goods and Services Tax 
collections “for December 2020 recorded all time high since implementation of GST”. Even as the economy is in 
what is called a technical recession, which translates into two quarters of negative growth, GST collections which 
averaged at around Rs 90,000 crore since August 2017, touched the new high of Rs 1.15,174 crore for the month 
of December. GST revenues grew even as the economy _______ (16) _______ (shrank, dwindled, aggrandized) 7.5 per 
cent in the July-Sept 2020 period. 

It is not just the Dalal Street vs Main Street picture or the GDP vs GST revenues imagery which are curious. 
Conventional economics ________ (17) _________ ( postulates, ionates, charges ) a correlation between inflation and 
interest rates. Consumer Price Inflation, which was under 3 per cent in 2017, has seen a secular rise now touching 
7.5 per cent. The RBI’s policy rates though have slid from over 6.5 per cent to 5.15 per cent in 2019 to 4 per cent 
in 2020. This column has previously highlighted the _________ (18) ________ ( phenomenon, movement, criteria ) 
affecting savers. Interest rates for depositors with money in a savings account halved to 3 per cent — a 365-day 
deposit now fetches what the savings account used to.

Q12. Choose the correct word to fill the blank (12).
explanation: The context implies the 'beginning' or 'start' of hope with the arrival of vaccines, making 'dawn' the appropriate metaphor.

subject: English Language
question:
(1) Mukesh Ambani’s Reliance is keen to sign a binding agreement.
(2) The agreement would be signed before next annual shareholders meeting, which is due to take place before \nthe end of April.

(A) However, the next annual _____________
(B) Before the next annual _______________
(C) Mukesh Ambani’s Reliance is ________________________
\nSelect the phrase/connector from the given three options which can be used to form a single sentence from the two sentences given below, implying the same meaning as expressed in the statement.
explanation: Option (B) 'Before the next annual...' allows the two sentences to be linked logically: 'Before the next annual shareholders meeting, which is due to take place before the end of April, Mukesh Ambani’s Reliance is keen to sign a binding agreement.'

subject: English Language
question:
(1) The bank plans to set aside Rs 20 crore to Rs 200 crore for the merger.
(2) It wants to run this merger scheme on a pilot basis at least eight months.

(A) In spite of the pilot basis ___________
(B) Despite the bank plans _______________
(C) Though the bank plans to ____________________
\nSelect the phrase/connector from the given three options which can be used to form a single sentence from the two sentences given below, implying the same meaning as expressed in the statement.
explanation: Option (C) 'Though the bank plans to...' correctly initiates a concessive clause that can link the bank's intent to set aside funds with the condition of running the scheme on a pilot basis.

subject: English Language
question:
Q21. (1) Social issues are distinguished from economic issues. 

(2) Some issues have both social and economic aspects. 

(A) Even though social issues are distinguished _____________

(B) Lest some issues _________________

(C) Moreover some issues ____________________
\nSelect the phrase/connector from the given three options which can be used to form a single sentence from the two sentences given below, implying the same meaning as expressed in the statement.
explanation: Option (A) creates a contrast using 'Even though', which appropriately bridges the distinction between social/economic issues mentioned in (1) and the overlap mentioned in (2).

subject: English Language
question:
Q22. (1) Within the sensex pack, 22 stocks gained ground

(2) Reliance Industries, HDFC Bank, ICICI Bank contributed the maximum to the day’s gains.

(A) Since, Reliance Industries _________________

(B) Within the sensex pack _________________

(C) Contradicting the sensex pack _____________
\nSelect the phrase/connector from the given three options which can be used to form a single sentence from the two sentences given below, implying the same meaning as expressed in the statement.
explanation: Option (B) serves as a logical connector by starting with the broader context ('Within the sensex pack') and following it with the specific details of the contributing stocks, maintaining the original meaning.

subject: English Language
question:
Q23. (1) SEBI has amended the Depository and Participants Regulations.

(2) It includes an additional explanation that states that ‘pledge’ would also refer to ‘re-pledge’ of securities for margin or settlement obligations.

(A) SEBI wants to amend the ______________

(B) By including an additional ________________

(C) In spite of amending _______________
\nSelect the phrase/connector from the given three options which can be used to form a single sentence from the two sentences given below, implying the same meaning as expressed in the statement.
explanation: Option (B) effectively links the action (amending) with the content of the amendment (the additional explanation), creating a coherent single sentence.

subject: English Language
question:
P. According to its developers, the key is labeling the nodes and identifying small lesions or growths that could be problematic.

2. It has an application in medical imaging that “detects lymph nodes in the human body in Computer Tomography (CT) images.
\nQ. AI tools are helping designers improve computational sophistication in health care.
\nR. What deep learning can do in this situation is train computers on data sets to learn what a normal-looking versus an irregular-appearing lymph node is.

5. If there were 10,000 images, the cost of this process would be $250,000, which is prohibitively expensive if done by humans.
\nS. Humans can do this, but radiologists charge $100 per hour and may be able to carefully read only four images an hour.
\nT. After doing that through imaging exercises and honing the accuracy of the labeling, radiological imaging specialists can apply this knowledge to actual patients and determine the extent to which someone is at risk of cancerous lymph nodes.
\nWhich of the following should be the FOURTH sentence after rearrangement? (1 Mark)
explanation: The logical sequence of the paragraph is: 1(Q), 2(given), 3(R), 4(P), 5(given), 6(S), 7(T). Q introduces AI in health care; sentence 2 describes a specific application; R explains how deep learning works for this; P details the developer's key approach; 5 explains the cost constraint; S contrasts human efficiency; T concludes with the result for patients. Thus, P is the fourth sentence.

subject: English Language
question:
P. According to its developers, the key is labeling the nodes and identifying small lesions or growths that could be problematic.

2. It has an application in medical imaging that “detects lymph nodes in the human body in Computer Tomography (CT) images.
\nQ. AI tools are helping designers improve computational sophistication in health care.
\nR. What deep learning can do in this situation is train computers on data sets to learn what a normal-looking versus an irregular-appearing lymph node is.

5. If there were 10,000 images, the cost of this process would be $250,000, which is prohibitively expensive if done by humans.
\nS. Humans can do this, but radiologists charge $100 per hour and may be able to carefully read only four images an hour.
\nT. After doing that through imaging exercises and honing the accuracy of the labeling, radiological imaging specialists can apply this knowledge to actual patients and determine the extent to which someone is at risk of cancerous lymph nodes.
\nWhich of the following should be the THIRD sentence after rearrangement? (1 Mark)
explanation: Based on the logical sequence Q -> 2 -> R -> P -> 5 -> S -> T, the third sentence is R.

subject: English Language
question:
Amid the solace (A) of COVID-19- induced (B) lockdown, the considerable improvement in the city’s air quality gave some challenges (C) for Delhiites fighting a contagion (D) from the confines of their homes. (1 Mark)
\nChoose the option which displays correct swapping of words making the sentence grammatically correct and contextually meaningful.
explanation: The word 'challenges' at (C) is contextually incorrect in relation to the improvement of air quality, and 'solace' at (A) is better placed at (C). The sentence should read: 'Amid the challenges of COVID-19-induced lockdown, the considerable improvement in the city’s air quality gave some solace for Delhiites...'

subject: English Language
question:
The Minister said the Railways was also route (A) “metro-like” trains on the corridor (B) between Palwal and Sonipat for commuters (C) travelling daily on the mulling (D)for jobs and business. (1 Mark)
\nChoose the option which displays correct swapping of words making the sentence grammatically correct and contextually meaningful.
explanation: The verb 'route' is incorrectly placed at (A) and 'mulling' (which refers to thinking over) is incorrectly placed at (D). Swapping them corrects the sentence to: 'The Minister said the Railways was also mulling “metro-like” trains on the corridor between Palwal and Sonipat for commuters travelling daily on the route for jobs and business.'

subject: English Language
question:
The salient seal (A) of these masks are that (B) they provide a complete features (C) around the nose and mouth 
with a gasket (D) fitting that provides no leakage. (1 Mark) 
 
In the following questions, four words are highlighted in bold and numbered as (A), (B), (C) and (D). Some words 
have been swapped from their places and interchanged with other words which are highlighted and labelled. 
Choose the option which displays correct swapping of words making the sentence grammatically correct and 
contextually meaningful. If all words are in their places, choose option (E) ‘’No Correction Required’’ as your 
answer.
explanation: The word 'features' should be at (A) as 'features of these masks', and 'seal' should be at (C) as 'complete seal'. Swapping A and C makes the sentence: 'The features of these masks are that they provide a complete seal around the nose and mouth with a gasket fitting that provides no leakage.'

subject: English Language
question:
The fillip (A) of an elderly Nagaland couple with stigma (B) has given a recovery (C) to the State government’s 
fight against the comorbidities (D) surrounding the novel coronavirus infection. (1 Mark) 
 
In the following questions, four words are highlighted in bold and numbered as (A), (B), (C) and (D). Some words 
have been swapped from their places and interchanged with other words which are highlighted and labelled. 
Choose the option which displays correct swapping of words making the sentence grammatically correct and 
contextually meaningful. If all words are in their places, choose option (E) ‘’No Correction Required’’ as your 
answer.
explanation: Swapping (A) 'fillip' with (C) 'recovery' and (B) 'stigma' with (D) 'comorbidities' makes the sentence meaningful: 'The recovery of an elderly Nagaland couple with comorbidities has given a fillip to the State government’s fight against the stigma surrounding the novel coronavirus infection.'

subject: English Language
question:
A number of scientists in the country think that they are on the ____________ of a major _____________. (1 Mark) 
 
In each of the following sentences there are two blank spaces. Below each five pairs of words have been denoted 
by numbers (A), (B), (C), (D) and (E). Find out which pair of words can be filled up in the blanks in the sentences 
in the same sequence to make the sentence meaningfully complete.
explanation: The phrase 'on the threshold/gateway of a major breakthrough' is idiomatic and fits perfectly in the given context.

subject: English Language
question:
You must _____________ your house in order before you ________________ to offer advice to others. (1 Mark) 
 
In each of the following sentences there are two blank spaces. Below each five pairs of words have been denoted 
by numbers (A), (B), (C), (D) and (E). Find out which pair of words can be filled up in the blanks in the sentences 
in the same sequence to make the sentence meaningfully complete.
explanation: The correct idiom is 'set your house in order', and 'venture to offer' completes the sentence logically.

subject: English Language
question:
Further, to augment bond market liquidity, corporates need to be encouraged to _____________ exiting bonds under 
the same International Securities Identification Number, to duly shore up floating _____________. (1 Mark) 
 
In each of the following sentences there are two blank spaces. Below each five pairs of words have been denoted 
by numbers (A), (B), (C), (D) and (E). Find out which pair of words can be filled up in the blanks in the sentences 
in the same sequence to make the sentence meaningfully complete.
explanation: The sentence refers to reissuing bonds to increase liquidity and shore up 'stocks' or 'stakes' (though 'stocks' is more common, 'reissue' is the correct verb for adding to existing bonds). Based on the provided options in the paper, 'reissue' and 'stocks' (Option E) is the correct pair.

subject: English Language
question: The internal and concurrent audit system of banks is intended to red _____________ risks in real time, but has failed and must be _____________ up.
explanation: The phrase 'red flag' means to identify or draw attention to a problem/risk. 'Shored up' means to support or strengthen. Therefore, 'red flag' and 'shored up' make the sentence contextually correct.

subject: English Language
question:
We need to go back to the drawing board on this project as it had some fundamental flaws from the start.
\nChoose the alternative which explains the meaning of the phrase 'go back to the drawing board' correctly without altering the meaning of the sentence.

option.A: We need to carry out a follow-up on this project as it had some fundamental flaws from the start.
option.B: We need to consult the experts on this project as it had some fundamental flaws from the start.
option.C: We need to revise the project from the beginning as it had some fundamental flaws from the start.
option.D: We need to endure the strategy on this project as it had some fundamental flaws from the start.

explanation: To 'go back to the drawing board' means to start over because a previous attempt failed or was flawed.

subject: English Language
question:
What the president had achieved during his presidency was a far cry from what he had promised during his run for election.
\nChoose the alternative which explains the meaning of the phrase 'a far cry from' correctly without altering the meaning of the sentence.

option.A: What the president had achieved during his presidency was a much crucial system of what he had promised during his run for election.
option.B: What the president had achieved during his presidency was much allied with what he had promised during his run for election.
option.C: What the president had achieved during his presidency was a pre-requisite for what he had promised during his run for election.
option.D: What the president had achieved during his presidency was very different from what he had promised during his run for election.

explanation: The idiom 'a far cry from' means something that is very different from another thing.

question:
Twelve persons were born on two dates either 7th or 16th of six different months of a year viz. January, March, June, July, September, and October but not necessarily in the same order. The persons whose name starts with a letter before the letter ’M’ were born in the first half of the year and the persons whose name starts with a letter after the letter ’M’ were born in the second half of the year. All letters are according to the English alphabet series. Example- If a person name is Rajesh then he will be born in the second half of the year. The person whose name consists of even number of letters after counting the number of letters is born on an odd numbered date and Vice-Versa. Example- If a person name is ‘Ramesh’ then he will be born on an odd number date (i.e 7) because the sum of number of letters is 6.
\nOnly two persons were born between Pranav and Kumar. The number of persons born before Gaurav is same as born after Yaati. Only four persons were born between Kunal and Gaurav. The number of persons born between Kunal and Sriti is one more than the persons born between Puja and Ram. Ayush was born before Kishan and Gopi. Puja was born in the month of having 31 days. Not more than two persons were born between Kishan and Ram. Swathi is one of the persons.
\nQ1. The number of persons born between Yaati and Sriti is same as persons born between ____ and ____?
explanation: Based on the rules provided, the birth dates and months are determined by the alphabetical order of names (first/second half of the year) and the letter count (even/odd dates). By mapping the constraints provided, the sequence of births is finalized. After solving the puzzle, it is found that the specific comparison asked in the question does not match the provided options.

question:
Directions (11-13): Read the following information carefully and answer the following questions.
\nEight persons P, Q, R, S, T, U, V, and W lives in a building of eight floors such that the topmost floor is numbered as 8 and the lowermost floor is numbered as 1 but not necessarily in the same order. They shops on eight different e-commerce websites viz. Flipkart, Ajio, Myntra, Jabong, Amazon, Shopclues, Jiomart, and Tata Cliq but not necessarily in the same order.
\nThe one who shops on Flipkart lives immediately above the one who shops on Shopclues. Neither V nor P shops on Flipkart, Tata Cliq and Myntra. Three persons live between V and S, who lives below V and also does not shops on Jabong. Only three persons live between W and R, who shops on Amazon. R lives on one of the even numbered floor above W and Q, who does not shops on Flipkart and Myntra and also does not live on the second floor. Two persons live between Q and the one who shops on Jabong. Three persons live between the one who shops on Jabong and the one who shops on Ajio. The number of persons living between the one who shops on Jabong and R is same as the number of persons living between the one who shops on Ajio and P. T lives on one of the floors below U. Both W and Q live on the even numbered floor.

11. Who among the following lives on the fourth floor?

option.C: The one who shops on Shopclues

explanation: Based on the constraints: R (Amazon) is at floor 6, W is at floor 2 (even, below R). Q is at floor 4 (even, not Flipkart/Myntra). Since Q is at floor 4 and the one who shops on Shopclues is at floor 4 or relevant adjacent positions, the logic leads to Q and the Shopclues shopper residing there. Therefore, both (b) and (c) are correct.

question:
Directions (14-17): Read the following information carefully and answer the following questions.
\nSeven persons A, B, C, D, E, F, and G are born on seven different dates in a year but not necessarily in the same order and they like different fruits. There is a gap of three days between the birth dates of every person. Ex- If R was born on 12th May then the person born immediately after R and immediately before R should be born a gap of 3 days. F was born on 16th June. Only two persons were born before F. The number of persons born before D, who likes Apple is equal to the number of persons born after G, who likes Guava. Only one person was born between G and A, who likes Oranges. B does not like Kiwi was born one of the dates before E but after C. B was not born after A. E does not like Grapes, Peach, and Kiwi. F does not like Peach, Kiwi, and Pear.

14. Who among the following was born on 28th June?
explanation: Based on the 3-day gap rule starting from the known date (F = 16th June), the dates are: 4th June, 8th June, 12th June, 16th June, 20th June, 24th June, 28th June. Mapping the persons reveals that E was born on 28th June and likes Pear.

(ii) A % B means A is 4m South of B. 
(iii) A * B means A is 6m North of B. 
(iv) A @ B means A is 4m East of B. 

30. In expression F&G, B&A%C&D&E*F@H, the Point A is \nin which direction of Point G?
explanation: By decoding the symbols and mapping the relative positions of points F, G, B, A, C, D, E, and H, we find that Point A is to the North-East of Point G.

subject: Reasoning
question:
If 
(i) P & Q means P is the brother Q. 
(ii) P % Q means P is the father of Q. 
(iii) P * Q means P is the sister of Q. 
(iv) P @ Q means P is the Son of Q. 
(v) P $ Q means P is the daughter Q. 
(vi) P ? Q means P is the mother of Q. 

31. In expression Q&G, G&A%C&D?E*F@H@R, then, how \nis F related to A?
explanation: Following the relations: Q is brother of G, G is brother of A, A is father of C, C is brother of D, D is mother of E, E is sister of F, F is son of H, H is son of R. Since E is daughter of D and F is brother/sister of E, F is child of D. D is child of A. Therefore, F is grandchild of A. Gender of F is given by F@H (F is son of H), but the linkage indicates F is either grandson or granddaughter depending on mapping.

subject: Reasoning
question:
If 
(i) P & Q means P is the brother Q. 
(ii) P % Q means P is the father of Q. 
(iii) P * Q means P is the sister of Q. 
(iv) P @ Q means P is the Son of Q. 
(v) P $ Q means P is the daughter Q. 
(vi) P ? Q means P is the mother of Q. 

32. In expression Q?P, P&A%W&D?E*F@H@R, then, how \nis Q related to the E’s mother?
explanation: Q is the mother of P. P is the brother of A. A is the father of W. W is the brother of D. D is the mother of E. Thus, D is E's mother. Q is the grandmother of D through the line of P and A (as P is the paternal uncle of W/D). Therefore, Q is the paternal grandmother of D. Wait, re-evaluating the chain, Q is the mother of P, P is the father of W and D, so Q is the paternal grandmother of D (E's mother). Given the options, 'Paternal Grandmother' is correct.

Technology has brought about transparency and 
is being used to achieve great heights across 
sectors in India in Modi governance.
explanation: To 'abrogate' the statement means to repeal or overturn it. Therefore, none of the provided choices abrogate the statement.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Statement- “In a big relief to liquor companies which are holding their stocks worth more than Rs. 200 crore in Bihar, the Supreme Court on Monday granted them two months more time to transport liquor out of the state and directed them to dispose off their stocks by July 31.”
I. Beverages Companies, contended that it was possible even to destroy the existing stocks by the end of this month.
II. The apex court had earlier fixed a deadline of May 31 for the companies to dispose off their stocks.
III. Seeking more time for transporting the stocks out of state, the confederation said in its petition that delay in disposing off stocks was beyond its control as clearances were required from various agencies.

Which of the following can be the Presupposition of the given statement?
explanation: A presupposition is an implicit assumption that must be true for the statement to make sense. Statement II provides the context for why the relief was needed (a previous deadline existed). Statement III explains the logic behind the petition for more time (the delay was beyond their control). Statement I contradicts the need for the relief, thus it is not a presupposition.

(I) The government in its Budget 2017 has already mandated seeding of Aadhaar number with Permanent Account Number (PAN) to avoid individuals using multiple PANs to evade taxes.
(II) Aadhaar is more secure and reliable than PAN for bank account transactions.
(III) Aadhaar number is issued by the Unique Identification Authority of India (UIDAI).
\nWhich of the following statement can be concluded from the given statement?
explanation: The original statement talks about making Aadhaar mandatory for bank accounts and transactions above 50,000. Option I refers to Budget 2017 and PAN, which is external information not concluded by the statement. Option II is a subjective claim not supported by the statement. Option III is factual information about UIDAI that is not a conclusion of the government's mandate mentioned.

subject: Quantitative Aptitude
question:
A train running between four stations i.e. A, B, C & D on Monday and Tuesday. The average speed of train on Monday during whole journey is 50 kmph and average speed of train on Tuesday during whole journey is 62.5 kmph. On Monday train takes one hour less to cover distance between A and B as compare to that of on Tuesday. Train takes equal time to cover distance between B to C on both the given days, while train takes three hours more to cover distance between C and D as compare to that of on Tuesday. The distance between A and B is 40% less than that of between C and D. The distance between B and C is 50% more than that of between A and B.\nNote - There is not any halt or stoppage from the station A to D.
\nQ46. Find the total time taken by train to cover distance from A to D on Tuesday?
explanation: Let distances be $d_{AB}$, $d_{BC}$, $d_{CD}$. Relations: $d_{AB} = 0.6 d_{CD}$ and $d_{BC} = 1.5 d_{AB} = 0.9 d_{CD}$. Total distance $D = d_{CD}(0.6 + 0.9 + 1) = 2.5 d_{CD}$. Using speeds ($V_{Mon}=50, V_{Tue}=62.5$) and time differences, solve the system of equations. Result for total time on Tuesday is 8 hours.

\nThere are two schools A & B in a town. Total number of girls in school B are 30 more than twice of total number of girls in school A. Total number of boys in school B are equal to total number of students (boys + girls) in school A.

50. If in school A total number of boys and girls are ‘X’ and ‘Y’ respectively, then the difference between total boys in both schools and total girls in both schools will be represented as (Total boys in both schools together are more than that of total girls):
explanation:
School A: Boys = $X$, Girls = $Y$. Total students = $X+Y$.
School B: Girls = $2Y + 30$, Boys = $X + Y$.
Total Boys = $X + (X + Y) = 2X + Y$.
Total Girls = $Y + (2Y + 30) = 3Y + 30$.
Difference = $(2X + Y) - (3Y + 30) = 2X - 2Y - 30$.

explanation:

subject: Quantitative Aptitude
question: 62. A bag kept some green balls, some blue balls and seven red balls. Probability of picking one green ball is $\frac{1}{7}$ more than that of picking one red ball from the bag and probability of picking one blue ball is $\frac{9}{35}$ more than that of picking one red ball from the bag. Find total number of blue balls in the bag?
explanation: Let $T$ be total balls, $R=7$, $P(R) = \frac{7}{T}$. $P(G) = \frac{7}{T} + \frac{1}{7}$, $P(B) = \frac{7}{T} + \frac{9}{35}$. Since $P(G)+P(B)+P(R)=1$, we have $(\frac{7}{T} + \frac{1}{7}) + (\frac{7}{T} + \frac{9}{35}) + \frac{7}{T} = 1$. $\frac{21}{T} + \frac{5}{35} + \frac{9}{35} = 1 \Rightarrow \frac{21}{T} + \frac{14}{35} = 1 \Rightarrow \frac{21}{T} + 0.4 = 1 \Rightarrow \frac{21}{T} = 0.6 \Rightarrow T = 35$. Number of blue balls $B = T \times P(B) = T \times (\frac{7}{35} + \frac{9}{35}) = 35 \times \frac{16}{35} = 16$. Actually, solving for blue: $P(B) = \frac{B}{T} = \frac{7}{35} + \frac{9}{35} = \frac{16}{35}$. So $B=16$.

subject: Quantitative Aptitude
question: 63. Aman born three years after marriage of his parents and Aman’s mother is three years younger than his father. At present Aman is 20 years younger than his mother and after three years ratio of the age of Aman’s father to his mother’s will be 11 : 10. Find how many years before his parents got married?
explanation: Let Father=$F$, Mother=$M$, Aman=$A$. $M=F-3$. $A=M-20 = F-23$. After 3 years: $(F+3)/(M+3) = 11/10$. $10F+30 = 11M+33$. Substitute $M=F-3$: $10F+30 = 11(F-3)+33 \Rightarrow 10F+30 = 11F-33+33 \Rightarrow F=30$. Then $M=27$, $A=7$. Aman was born 7 years ago. Aman was born 3 years after marriage, so marriage was $7+3 = 10$ years ago.

question:
Direction (65 – 67): A man is going shop from his home with the speed of _______ kmph and time taken to reach the shop by him is _______ hours. After reaching there he purchases a cylindrical jar of certain height having capacity equal to 83259 cm$^3$. The man also purchases a conical vessel whose capacity is $\frac{1}{27}$th of cylindrical jar and height of conical vessel is 14 cm.\nNote: Height of conical vessel is four times of the height of cylindrical jar.

65. Find the ratio of the radius of cylindrical jar to the radius of conical vessel?
explanation: Cylindrical volume $V_c = 83259$. Conical volume $V_{cone} = V_c / 27$. Height of conical vessel $h_{cone} = 14$. Height of cylinder $h_{cyl} = 14/4 = 3.5$. $V_{cone} = \frac{1}{3} \pi r_{cone}^2 h_{cone}$. $V_c = \pi r_{cyl}^2 h_{cyl}$. Ratio $\frac{V_c}{V_{cone}} = 27 = \frac{\pi r_{cyl}^2 h_{cyl}}{\frac{1}{3} \pi r_{cone}^2 h_{cone}} = 3 \cdot \frac{r_{cyl}^2}{r_{cone}^2} \cdot \frac{3.5}{14} = 3 \cdot \frac{r_{cyl}^2}{r_{cone}^2} \cdot \frac{1}{4} = \frac{3}{4} (r_{cyl}/r_{cone})^2$. $27 \times 4 / 3 = (r_{cyl}/r_{cone})^2 \Rightarrow 36 = (r_{cyl}/r_{cone})^2 \Rightarrow r_{cyl}/r_{cone} = 6$. Wait, check values. $\frac{83259}{V_{cone}} = 27$. Ratio $r_{cyl}/r_{cone} = 6:1$.

\nDirection (66 – 67): The distance (in km) between the home and shop in numerical value is equal to seven more than $\frac{1}{3}$rd of square root of $\frac{1}{11}$th of the capacity of the cylindrical jar and the speed of the man is four times of the time taken by him to reach the shop.

66. Speed of man _________kmph.
explanation: Distance $D = 7 + \frac{1}{3} \sqrt{83259/11} = 7 + \frac{1}{3} \sqrt{7569} = 7 + \frac{1}{3} (87) = 7 + 29 = 36$ km. Speed $S = 4 \times Time (T)$. $D = S \times T = (4T) \times T = 4T^2$. $36 = 4T^2 \Rightarrow T^2 = 9 \Rightarrow T = 3$. $S = 4 \times 3 = 12$ kmph.

subject: English Language
question:
Alas, we also find that goods demand is back at pre-pandemic levels and may not be the key driver of a continued rebound. Thankfully, pent-up services demand can play that role. Still 25% below normal, services can get a shot in the arm as herd immunity rises, in part led by vaccine roll-out. GDP growth is likely to be strong for the next few quarters, rising from -6.3% y-o-y in FY21 to 11.2% y-o-y in FY22.
\nBut then, what next? By definition, pent-up demand is a one-time driver of growth. Once services demand is back at pre-pandemic levels, say by end 2021, what will drive growth? It is possible that the scars the pandemic leaves behind will begin to show up around that same time, presenting a double whammy for growth. And this is where the centre stepped in with the budget. It tried to introduce a new narrative for medium-term growth, namely capital expenditure. In particular, it introduced the following:
\nThe capex budget was raised by 0.8% of GDP over two years (FY21 and FY22). In fact, only after adjusting for the higher capex multipliers is the FY22 fiscal impulse positive.
\nThe government did not impose any new taxes/cesses, nor did it make changes in capital gains tax. Our previous work has shown that policy stability tends to crowd-in private sector capex.
\nThe government outline plans to create two new institutions, a bad bank and a DFI, although much will depend on the design and implementation over time.
\nOn Feb 5, RBI outlined its role in this new narrative–not being the main driver of growth as it was in 2020, but playing a supportive role and helping it through its larger-than-expected market borrowing.
\nRBI will have to tread the fine line between normalising liquidity (especially with inflation likely to be north of the 4% target over the next year) and maintaining orderly conditions in the bond and FX markets. Liquidity switching could help. For instance, it could use the space freed up by the reversal in CRR cut for bond purchases. Or, in the face of a rising trade deficit and falling BoP surplus, it could focus more on bond purchases than dollar purchases.RBI is expected to start raising the reverse repo rate in 2H2021, the repo rate may remain unchanged at 4% over the foreseeable future, doing its bit for keeping interest rates as low as possible.
\nQ81. According to the passage, what can be said about the economy after the pandemic?

option.A: New cases have risen, and economic activity is highly influenced by it.
option.B: Still 85% below normal, services can get a shot in the arm as herd immunity rises, in part led by vaccine roll-out.
option.C: Economic activity is racing back to pre-pandemic levels.

explanation: The passage states that services demand is 'Still 25% below normal', not 85%. Furthermore, while the passage notes goods demand is back at pre-pandemic levels, it does not suggest economic activity is universally racing back; it emphasizes that pent-up services demand is the key driver moving forward. Therefore, none of the options A, B, or C are factually correct based on the text.

subject: English Language
question:
Alas, we also find that goods demand is back at pre-pandemic levels and may not be the key driver of a continued rebound. Thankfully, pent-up services demand can play that role. Still 25% below normal, services can get a shot in the arm as herd immunity rises, in part led by vaccine roll-out. GDP growth is likely to be strong for the next few quarters, rising from -6.3% y-o-y in FY21 to 11.2% y-o-y in FY22.
\nBut then, what next? By definition, pent-up demand is a one-time driver of growth. Once services demand is back at pre-pandemic levels, say by end 2021, what will drive growth? It is possible that the scars the pandemic leaves behind will begin to show up around that same time, presenting a double whammy for growth. And this is where the centre stepped in with the budget. It tried to introduce a new narrative for medium-term growth, namely capital expenditure. In particular, it introduced the following:
\nThe capex budget was raised by 0.8% of GDP over two years (FY21 and FY22). In fact, only after adjusting for the higher capex multipliers is the FY22 fiscal impulse positive.
\nThe government did not impose any new taxes/cesses, nor did it make changes in capital gains tax. Our previous work has shown that policy stability tends to crowd-in private sector capex.
\nThe government outline plans to create two new institutions, a bad bank and a DFI, although much will depend on the design and implementation over time.
\nOn Feb 5, RBI outlined its role in this new narrative–not being the main driver of growth as it was in 2020, but playing a supportive role and helping it through its larger-than-expected market borrowing.
\nRBI will have to tread the fine line between normalising liquidity (especially with inflation likely to be north of the 4% target over the next year) and maintaining orderly conditions in the bond and FX markets. Liquidity switching could help. For instance, it could use the space freed up by the reversal in CRR cut for bond purchases. Or, in the face of a rising trade deficit and falling BoP surplus, it could focus more on bond purchases than dollar purchases.RBI is expected to start raising the reverse repo rate in 2H2021, the repo rate may remain unchanged at 4% over the foreseeable future, doing its bit for keeping interest rates as low as possible.
\nQ82. The line emboldened in the given passage may or may not have an error. If there is an error, find the part that contains the error or choose ‘no error’ as your answer.
\nThe government outline plans (A) / to create two new institutions, a bad bank and a DFI (B) / although much will depend on the design (C) /and implementation over time. (D)
explanation: The error is in part (A). 'The government outline plans' is grammatically incorrect. It should be 'The government outlines plans' (third-person singular verb required for the subject 'government').

\nQ84. Which of the following sentences is incorrect according to the passage?

option.A: The capex budget was raised by 0.8% of GDP over two years.
option.B: Thankfully, pent-up services demand can play that role. Still 35% below normal, services can get a shot in the arm as herd immunity rises, in part led by vaccine roll-out.
option.C: After a c.24% contraction in the quarter ending June 2020, we expect GDP to grow by a positive 1.8% in the quarter ending October 2020.

explanation: Sentence (b) is incorrect because the passage states services demand is 25% below normal, not 35%. Sentence (c) is incorrect because the passage does not contain the information regarding a 24% contraction or an expected 1.8% GDP growth in October 2020. Thus, both (b) and (c) are incorrect based on the provided text.

subject: English Language
subtopic: Water crisis and its socio-economic impacts
question:
away from the wheat-paddy cycle, farmers in the state 
have been adopting a decade-old scheme to utilise 
underground pipeline system for irrigation. 
 
In light of this crisis, Central government on its part has 
created a Jal Shakti Ministry under a full-fledged cabinet 
minister to resolve the water crisis but a lot more needs to 
be done. There is a huge dependence on monsoon rains to 
replenish most of India’s important water sources such as 
underground aquifers, lakes, rivers, and reservoirs. But 
monsoon is vulnerable to factors such as climate change, 
El-Nino, etc. 
 
Certain regions have surplus amounts of water for their 
need while others face perennial droughts for most of the 
year. For instance, Drought is a recurrent phenomenon in 
Andhra Pradesh where no district is entirely free of 
droughts. Rajasthan is one of the most drought-prone 
areas of India. 
 
Population growth, industrialization, rapid urbanisation, 
rising needs of irrigation and increase in domestic water 
usage have accelerated the demand for water. Since 
urbanization increases in India at a rapid pace = water 
demand will increase rapidly as city dwellers consume 
more water than rural people. 
 
Currently, about 285 million or 33% of India’s total 
population resides in urban areas. By 2050 this figure will 
reach 50%. Rapid urbanisation is adding to the water 
scarcity issue in the country. 
 
Presence of buildings, tar, and cement roads = even if a city 
like Mumbai gets good rains, the rainwater is not retained 
in the area as the water is not allowed to percolate 
underground. 
 
Therefore, water required for cities is largely drawn from 
neighbouring villages and far-off rivers and lakes = 
threatening the availability in those areas. 
 
Large cities also generate large quantities of urban sewage 
which pollutes the freshwater sources and ocean waters. 
However, only about 20% of urban wastewater is currently 
treated globally. In India, the figure is even lower. 
 
A Niti Aayog report predicted that water demand will be 
twice the present supply by 2030 and India could lose up 
to 6% of its GDP during that time. Water shortages are 
hurting India’s capacity to generate electricity because 
40% of thermal power plants are located in areas where 
water scarcity is high.Indian agriculture is heavily 
dependent on monsoon (not dependable) + Ineffective 
agricultural practices in irrigated areas = Water stress in 
agriculture = Poor Cultivation = Farmer suicides. Not only 
farmers are affected by the water crisis, urban dwellers in 
cities and towns across India are also facing a never seen 
before drinking water scarcity. In India, there are conflicts 
between Karnataka and Tamil Nadu over sharing of 
Cauvery waters, between Gujarat and Madhya Pradesh 
over sharing of Narmada waters, between Andhra Pradesh 
and Telangana over sharing of Krishna waters, etc. 

85. According to the passage, which of the following 
sentences indicate the impact of water crisis?

option.A: It is affecting the electricity generation capacity of \nIndia as the thermal power plants need water to \ngenerate electricity.
option.B: About 20% of urban wastewater is currently \ntreated globally which shows a lower figure,
option.C: Rising needs of irrigation and increase in domestic \nwater usage have accelerated water crisis in India.
option.D: It has affected the coal mines in India and \nproduction of coal has reduced drastically.

explanation: The passage explicitly states that water shortages are hurting India’s capacity to generate electricity because 40% of thermal power plants are located in areas where water scarcity is high.

86. Choose the word which is most opposite to the term 
‘vulnerable’ highlighted in the passage?
explanation: The term 'vulnerable' means susceptible to physical or emotional attack or harm. 'Protected' is the most appropriate opposite in this context.

subject: English Language
subtopic: World Bank vs. China development models in Africa
question:
The government has taken pains to ensure that the electricity grids and transportation networks reach every village in China. 
\nChina is prepared to help Africa in substantial ways in agriculture, roads, power, health and education. And that is not an empty boast. Chinese leaders are prepared to share new high yield rice varieties, with their African counterparts and, all over Africa, China is financing and constructing basic infrastructure. 
\nThis illustrates what is wrong with the World Bank. The World Bank has often forgotten the most basic lessons of development, preferring to lecture the poor and force them to privatise basic infrastructure, which is untenable, rather than to help the poor to invest in infrastructure and other crucial sectors. The Banks’s failure began in the early 1980s when under the ideological sway of them American President and British Prime Minister it tried to get Africa and other poor regions to cut back or close down government investments and services. 
\nFor 25 years, the bank tries to get governments out of agriculture, leaving impoverished peasants to fend for themselves. The result has been a disaster in Africa, with farm productivity stagnant for decades. The bank also pushed for privatization of national health systems, water utilities, and road and power networks, and has grossly underfinanced these critical sectors. 
\nThis extreme free-market ideology, also called “structural adjustment”, went against the practical lessons of development successes in China and the rest or Asia. Practical development strategy recognizes that public investments - in agriculture, health, education, and infrastructure- are necessary complements to private investments. The World Bank has instead wrongly seen such vital public investments as an enemy of private sector development. Whenever the banks’ ideology failed, it has blamed the poor for corruption, mismanagement, or lack of initiative. 
\nInstead of focusing its attention on helping the poorest countries to improve their infrastructure, there has been a crusade against corruption. The good news is that African governments are getting the message on how to spur economic growth and are getting crucial help from China and other partners that are less wedded to extreme free-market ideology than the world Bank. 
\nThey have declared their intention to invest in infrastructure, agriculture modernization, public health, and education. It is clear the Bank can regain its relevance only if it becomes practical once again, by returning its focus to financing public investments in priority sectors. If that happens, the Bank can still do justice to the bold vision of a world of shared prosperity that prompted its creation after World War II.
\nQ88. Which of the following statements can be inferred from the passage?

option.A: Criticize the World Bank for playing a crucial role in China’s development but neglecting Australia.
option.B: Make a case for the closure of the World Bank since it promotes US interests over those of other countries.
option.C: Illustrate how China can play a more concrete role in Africa.
option.D: Use China’s success as an example of the changes required in World ideology.

explanation: The passage highlights China's successful development model as a contrast to the failed World Bank ideology, suggesting the Bank needs to adopt similar practical strategies of public investment to regain relevance.

option.A: Reduce the influence of the Africa and Britain in its functioning.

option.D: Support China’s involvement in developing Africa.

subject: English Language
question:
contraction in the wake of the pandemic, while the 
benchmark Sensex has shot up from around 29,000 points 
to over 47,000 points. How has the fall in growth impacted 
government _______ (95) _______ (expenditure, asset, 
revenues)? In theory, drop in output would result in drop 
in revenues. That though is not necessarily true in the 
Indian context. This week, the government informed India 
that the Goods and Services Tax collections “for December 
2020 recorded all time high since implementation of GST”. 
Even as the economy is in what is called a technical 
recession, which translates into two quarters of negative 
growth, GST collections which averaged at around Rs 
90,000 crore since August 2017, touched the new high of 
Rs 1.15,174 crore for the month of December. GST 
revenues grew even as the economy _______ (96) _______ 
(shrank, dwindled, aggrandized) 7.5 per cent in the July-
Sept 2020 period. 

It is not just the Dalal Street vs Main Street picture or the 
GDP vs GST revenues imagery which are curious. 
Conventional economics ________ (97) _________  (postulates, 
ionates, charges ) a correlation between inflation and 
interest rates. Consumer Price Inflation, which was under 
3 per cent in 2017, has seen a secular rise now touching 7.5 
per cent. The RBI’s policy rates though have slid from over 
6.5 per cent to 5.15 per cent in 2019 to 4 per cent in 2020. 
This column has previously highlighted the _________ (98) 
________ ( phenomenon, movement, criteria ) affecting 
savers. Interest rates for depositors with money in a 
savings account halved to 3 per cent — a 365-day deposit 
now fetches what the savings account used to. 

Q95. How has the fall in growth impacted government _______ (95) _______ (expenditure, asset, revenues)?
explanation: The passage discusses the impact of falling growth on government revenues. Specifically, it mentions how GST collections reached an all-time high despite a technical recession. Therefore, 'revenues' is the correct contextually appropriate term.

6.5 per cent to 5.15 per cent in 2019 to 4 per cent in 2020. 
This column has previously highlighted the _________ (98) 
________ ( phenomenon, movement, criteria ) affecting 
savers. Interest rates for depositors with money in a 
savings account halved to 3 per cent — a 365-day deposit 
now fetches what the savings account used to. 

Q97. Conventional economics ________ (97) _________ (postulates, ionates, charges ) a correlation between inflation and interest rates.
explanation: To 'postulate' means to suggest or assume the existence, fact, or truth of something as a basis for reasoning, which fits the context of how economic theories describe correlations.
